coredata.dc:title: In situ synthesis and enhanced visible light photocatalytic activity of C-TiO2 microspheres/carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Ruidi
Li, Hui
Duan, Libing
Shen, Hao
Zhang, Yangyang
Zhao, Xiaoru
coredata.dc:description: TiO2 microspheres and TiO2/carbon quantum dots (CQDs) composites with different CQDs contents were successfully synthesized via solvothermal and in situ hydrothermal method. The structure and morphology of the prepared samples were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and transmission electron microscope (TEM). Results showed that carbon elements were successfully doped into the TiO2 lattice (C-TiO2) and CQDs were hybrid with C-TiO2 microspheres. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS), valence band XPS (VB-XPS) and UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) analyses revealed that carbon doped into TiO2 microspheres could lead to local energy levels in the band structure and generate valence band tails to absorb visible light. The photocatalytic activities of these samples were evaluated by the photodegradation of Rhodamine B (RhB) under visible light irradiation. C-TiO2/CQDs samples presented an enhanced photocatalytic performance compared with pristine TiO2, which could be attributed to the present of CQDs, acting as adsorption sites for RhB molecules and charge separation centers to impede the recombination and prolong the life time of electron and hole pairs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: C-TiO2 microspheres
Carbon quantum dot (CQD)
Carbon-doped
Photocatalytic activity under visible light

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issfirst issn issnnorm issuelist itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure body acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid primabst ref

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of the as-prepared TiO2 and TiO2/CQDs hybrids with different CQDs loading amounts.
SEM image (a) and EDS(b) of the TiO2 microspheres. SEM images of TiO2/CQDs hybrids (c-f): TiO2/CQDs-1, TiO2/CQDs-2, TiO2/CQDs-3 and TiO2/CQDs-4, respectively.
Photodegradation of RhB by different samples under visible light irradiation (a), and variations of ln(C/C0) versus irradiation time with different samples (b).
TEM image (a) and high resolution TEM image (b) of TiO2/CQDs-2 sample.
FT-IR of as-prepared TiO2 and TiO2/CQDs-2.
XPS survey scan of as-prepared TiO2 and TiO2/CQDs-2(a), and high resolution XPS scan of Ti2p(b), C 1s(c), O 1s(d) over as-prepared TiO2 and TiO2/CQDs-2 samples.
Valence band XPS spectra of C-TiO2 (a) and C-TiO2/CQDs-2(b); the calculated band structures of C-TiO2 (a) and C-TiO2/CQDs-2(b).
The UV–vis diffuse reflectance absorption spectra of P25, C-TiO2 and C-TiO2/CQDs-2.
Band structure of the C-TiO2 and schematic of the separation and transfer of photogenerated charges in the C-TiO2/CQDs samples.

head.ce:title: In situ synthesis and enhanced visible light photocatalytic activity of C-TiO2 microspheres/carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Space Applied Physics and Chemistry, Ministry of Education of China; School of Science, Northwestern Polytechnical University, Xi’an 710072, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Space Applied Physics and Chemistry, Ministry of Education of China; School of Science, Northwestern Polytechnical University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: TiO2 microspheres and TiO2/carbon quantum dots (CQDs) composites with different CQDs contents were successfully synthesized via solvothermal and in situ hydrothermal method. The structure and morphology of the prepared samples were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and transmission electron microscope (TEM). Results showed that carbon elements were successfully doped into the TiO2 lattice (C-TiO2) and CQDs were hybrid with C-TiO2 microspheres. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS), valence band XPS (VB-XPS) and UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) analyses revealed that carbon doped into TiO2 microspheres could lead to local energy levels in the band structure and generate valence band tails to absorb visible light. The photocatalytic activities of these samples were evaluated by the photodegradation of Rhodamine B (RhB) under visible light irradiation. C-TiO2/CQDs samples presented an enhanced photocatalytic performance compared with pristine TiO2, which could be attributed to the present of CQDs, acting as adsorption sites for RhB molecules and charge separation centers to impede the recombination and prolong the life time of electron and hole pairs.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years，the expanding organic environmental pollution has become the crucial issue which not only influences human being's health, but also impedes the development of society and economy [1]. Therefore, a great amount of semiconductors(such as TiO2 [2], ZnO [3], SnO2 [4], ZnS, CdS [5] and MoS2 [6]) have been carried out as efficient photocatalysts in environmental purification and energy conversion due to their unique chemical characters and electronic structures [7]. Among these semiconductors, titanium oxide (TiO2), a n-type semiconductor with the band gap of about 3.2eV at room temperature, has attracted much attention and becomes one of the most promising candidates for dye degradation [8–10]. Moreover, TiO2 was synthesized into microsphere shape to improve its photocatalytic performance, for this kind of structure has uniform morphologies with less agglomeration and large surface areas [11–13]. However, the poor solar energy absorption and rapid recombination of photo-generated electrons(e-) and holes(h+) greatly limited the efficiency of photocatalytic activities [9,14]. Thus, energy band engineering such as doping with metal [15] or nonmetal elements [9]was used to guarantee the absorption of the visible light. Meanwhile, hybrid and hetero-structure control such as modifying with noble metals [11], coupling with other semiconductors [6,16] and supporting with other materials [17,18] were investigated to restrain the recombination of electron and hole pairs, respectively. Among these techniques, coupling TiO2 with carbon materials has been deemed to be a valid approach [8,18,19].

Carbon materials, including carbon nanotubes [20,21], grapheme [3,5,22], fullerene [23], amorphous carbon [18,24] and incorporated carbon [13]， have been used to achieve TiO2/carbon materials hetero-structures for the degradation of pollutants, CO2 conversion and water splitting in recent years. However, it is generally accepted that these carbon materials mentioned above also suffer the drawbacks of high cost and complex preparation processes. Recently, Fang et al. [13] provided a facile way to support carbon materials with TiO2 and suggested that carbon-incorporated TiO2 microspheres have enhanced visible light photocatalytic activity. They pave the road to seek new and desirable photocatalysts. But they had not further investigated the role of carbon-incorporated TiO2 on the influence of enhanced visible light degradation. In order to further enhance the photocatalytic performance, they decorated a narrow-bandgap semiconductor Cu2O to the carbon-incorporated TiO2. However, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with sizes below 10nm should be more reasonable, because they have little or no influence on the redox interface between TiO2 and the contaminants. Furthermore, CQDs showed both down and up-conversion fluorescences and also performed as electron reservoirs, as many researchers reported [25–27]. It should also be emphasized that the lack of interfacial bonding between CQDs and host catalysts could not benefit the transfer of photo-generated electrons. Herein, in situ hydrothermal synthesis should be taken into consideration. By this way CQDs can be anchored at TiO2 surfaces by chemical bond through –OH groups on its surface. Recently, to achieve natural visible light exposed photocatalytic activity, many attempts were made to obtain CQDs nanostructures with other semiconductors. Although lots of researches established excellent visible-light-driven photocatalytic activities when hybrid with CQDs and found an optimum concentration of CQDs to achieve the best performance, in most reported systems [14,19,27–29], the critical role of CQDs in the enhanced photo degradation has not been studied in detail, and the mechanism of the degradation has not been investigated yet.

In this work, TiO2 microspheres have been synthesized via a facile solvothermal process using the pre-prepared TiO2 precursor made by sol-gel method. The different concentrations of CQDs have been applied to modify the as-prepared TiO2 microspheres via a green in situ hydrothermal synthesis because the –OH groups act as the nucleation of the carbon dot. The hydrothermal method using glucose as precursor may greatly influence its surface chemical states and defects, which in turn determine the optical and photocatalytic properties of CQDs modified TiO2 microspheres [18]. Therefore, the optimum concentration of CQDs and its effect on the microstructure and photocatalytic properties of the hybrids have been investigated and discussed.

The TiO2 precursor was fabricated via a sol-gel method as follows. In detail, 13.60g of tetrabutyltitanate(TBOT), as the titanium source, was dissolved in absolute ethanol with 30min of magnetic stirring( marked as solution A); 0.60g of glacialaceticacid, 2.00g of acetylacetone(AcAc) and 4.32g of deionized water(DI) were put into some ethanol successively, and dispersed with ultrasonic for 10min ( noted as solution B); Then the solution B was added into solution A drop by drop. The mixture was aged for 24h after magnetic stirring for several hours, then dried at 80℃ for 12h, and was collected for further use.

TiO2 microspheres were prepared via a typical hydrothermal method. 0.3g as-prepared TiO2 precursor was dissolved into 60mL of 3wt% HCl/EtOH aqueous solution under continuous stirring. Then the obtained mixture solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave with 100mL capacity, which was heated at 150℃ for 6h. When cooled to the room temperature, the as-prepared yellowish products were washed with ethanol and DI water for several times and then centrifuged and dried at 80℃ for 12h. Finally, the as-prepared microspheres were obtained.

0.1g ground TiO2 microspheres were dispersed in 10mL DI water and stirred vigorously for 30min. Then 15mL aqueous solution of glucose (0.01wt%) was added into the suspension and stirred for 30min respectively. Then, the obtained mixed suspension was transferred into a 50mL Teflon-lined autoclave and maintained at 90°C for 4h. The following product was collected by centrifugation, washed with ethanol and DI water, and then dried as the same as the TiO2 microspheres. Other samples with different mass of glucose (0.02wt%, 0.05wt%, 0.1wt%) were fabricated using a similar method to obtain different concentrations of CQDs. The final products were noted as TiO2/CQDs-1, TiO2/CQDs-2, TiO2/CQDs-3 and TiO2/CQDs-4, corresponding to the glucose of 0.01wt%, 0.02wt%, 0.05% and 0.1wt%, respectively.

The photocatalytic measurements were carried out at the room temperature by the degradation of Rhodamine B (RhB) under visible light irradiation with a 500W Xeon-lamp equipped with a 420nm cut-off filter. In the experiment, 100mg of the products was dispersed into 50mL of 10mg/L RhB aqueous solution. Before light on, the suspension was kept in the dark under stirring for 30min to reach an adsorption-desorption equilibrium. During illumination, a circulation of water through an external cooling coil was conducted to maintain the temperature at about 25℃. After irradiation for an appropriate interval, the reaction solution was centrifuged and the concentration of RhB in solution was determined by an UV–vis spectrophotometer for calculating the photocatalytic degradation rate of RhB.

The products were analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD, PANalytical X′pert MPD PRO) using Cu Kαradiation(λ=0.154nm). The morphological and microstructural details of the samples were examined by field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM, JSM-7000F) and a transmission electron microscope (TEM, Tecnai F30 G2). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was acquired on a Bruker TENSOR27 FTIR spectrometer to measure different functional groups on the surface of the samples. X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS, Kratos AXIS Ultra DLD) was carried out to analyze surface chemical states of these composites， and all binding energies were calibrated with surface adventitious carbon of 284.6eV. The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) was recorded on a PE Lambda 950 spectrophotometer, in which BaSO4 was used as the reflectance standard material.

The crystal of TiO2 which is consisted of anatase or rutile may dramatically affect the photocatalytic activity and definitively determine its practical activity [30]. A systematic XRD study was carried out to analyze the crystal structure of the obtained samples and the results were shown in Fig. 1. The distinctive peaks at 2θ=25.3°, 37.8°, 48.1°, 53.9°, 55.1°, 62.7°, 68.8° and 70.3° can be indexed to the (101), (004), (200), (105), (211), (204), (116) and (220) planes of the anatase phase TiO2 (JCPDS#21–1272), respectively. No characteristic peak of carbon can be detected in the CQDs modified as-prepared TiO2 samples, which may be attributed to the low CQD content in these composites. In addition, no other peaks were detected within the instrument scope, which indicates the high purity of the as-prepared samples.

Fig. 2. presented the corresponding SEM images of the TiO2 microspheres and TiO2/CQDs composites in high magnifications. As shown in Fig. 2a, the diameter of the TiO2 is about 5µm in size with a uniform morphology and a well dispersion, and its surface is very smooth. The energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) analysis of the as-prepared TiO2 (Fig. 2b) showed that the sample consists of C, O, Ti elements, revealing that carbon has been doped into TiO2 microspheres. Furthermore, when glucose, which was chosen as the green precursor for carbon dot source, was added, the products instantly turned from white to yellow or brown color after hydrothermal treatment owing to the different glucose amounts, implying the yield of TiO2/CQDs composites. Fig. 2c-f showed the morphologies of the carbon dot modified TiO2 samples (TiO2/CQDs-1, TiO2/CQDs-2, TiO2/CQDs-3 and TiO2/CQDs-4). It can be seen that the surface morphology and surface area of TiO2 microspheres with the CQDs modifying do not change obviously.

The photocatalytic activities of the synthesized samples were evaluated by photodegradation of Rhodamine B (RhB) under visible light irradiation as shown in Fig. 3a. C0 is initial concentration of RhB while C is the corresponding concentration in real time. Considering that RhB may possibly self-degrade without catalyst, a blank experiment was established as a control line. Before light on, an adsorption-desorption equilibrium was carried out. The result of the performance revealed that all the obtained samples exhibited significant response to the visible light compared with the commercial TiO2 (P25). Moreover, in comparison with the as-prepared TiO2, the photocatalytic activities of hybrid samples were significantly improved, among these samples, TiO2/CQDs-2 showed the best performance: the photocatalytic activity of TiO2/CQDs-2 increased slightly when the mass ratio of carbon source (glucose) rose from 0.01%wt to 0.02%wt, and then clearly dropped when the mass ratio further increased to 0.05%. To have a better understanding of the reaction kinetics of the RhB degradation, the experimental data were evaluated by a pseudo-first-order kinetic model: ln(C/C0)=-kt, the rate of photodegradation could be described by the slope coefficient k. As displayed in Fig. 3b, the TiO2/CQDs-2 sample possessed the optimum value and was almost 3 times as high as that of as-prepared TiO2.

In order to further confirm the formation of the microstructure of the CQDs modified TiO2, TEM images were explored. As displayed in Fig. 4, it can be seen from the low magnification image (Fig. 4a) that some dark dots (marked by white circles) with an average size of several to dozens of nanometers are distributed on the relative microspheres, implying that CQDs are deposited on the surface of the as-prepared TiO2. Moreover, an obvious crystalline structure (Fig. 4b) was observed, with an interplanar spacing of 0.21nm which is assigned to in-plane (100) facet of disordered graphitic carbon [8,31]. The observed lattice constant of the TiO2 crystallite was detected to be 0.34nm corresponding to the (101) crystal plane of anatase TiO2 (JCPDS#21–1272). What's more, it can be seen that there are intimate interface combinations between TiO2 microsphere and CQDs, which may form close junctions and provide the possibility for charge transfer and separation, and this is beneficial to the photocatalytic performance.

Different functional groups on the surface of as-prepared TiO2 and TiO2/CQDs-2 samples were confirmed by FTIR spectrum, as shown in Fig. 5. The band over the range 3200–3600cm−1 is attributed to the O-H stretching of adsorbed water molecules and the surface hydroxyl groups on TiO2, which is considered to be crucial in the photocatalytic process, because these groups can capture the photo generated electrons and holes to produce reactive oxygen species [32,33]. The absorption band at 1630cm−1 and 1442cm−1are signed to stretching vibrations of C=C and C-O group respectively [17], indicating the residual C elemental in the catalyst surface. The presence of characteristic bands between 1000 and 4000cm−1corresponds to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration of C-O-C [27]. As Zhang et al. reported that pure P25 powder showed a low frequency band around 690cm−1 [34,35], the existence of the broad absorption below 1000cm−1 in the Fig. 5 is confirmed to the synergistic effect of a combination of Ti-O-Ti and Ti-O-C vibrations [36], which is consistent with the XPS result.

To investigate surface chemical composition and bonding configuration of the as-prepared TiO2 and TiO2/CQDs-2 composites, the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed and the results were shown in Fig. 6. The full-scale XPS pattern of samples was observed in Fig. 6a. When calibrated with surface adventitious carbon (C-C/C=C) of 284.6eV, peaks responding to Ti 3s,Ti 3p, Ti 2s, Ti 2p, C 1s and O 1s were founded in both TiO2 and TiO2/CQDs-2 composites. Fig. 6b-d illustrated the high-resolution XPS spectra of Ti 2p, C 1s and O 1s, respectively. Ti 2p peaks are located at 458.4eV and 464.1eV (Fig. 6b) with a spin-orbital doublet splitting (Ti 2p3/2-Ti 2p1/2) of 5.7eV in as-prepared TiO2 sample, which implies an oxidation state of Ti4+. As for the TiO2/CQDs-2 sample, the Ti 2p peaks exert a slight red shift of 0.2eV compared with those of TiO2, which conceivably indicates the coupling of TiO2 and carbon. It can be seen that C 1s peak of TiO2 could be resolved into three different parts using Shirley-type base line

with a combination of a Gaussian (80%) curve and a Lorentzian (20%) curve (G-L), which are assigned to C–C/C=C bond (284.6eV), C–O bond (286.2eV) and C=O (288.5eV), respectively. When TiO2 is modified with carbon, an additional peak located at 282.7eV is detected, which probably arise from the formation of Ti–C bonds [33]. To further investigate the oxide state, O 1s was measured (Fig. 6d). Three peaks were observed at the binding energies of 529.7eV, 531.5eV and 533.1eV in the spectrum of as-prepared TiO2 samples, which are thought to Ti–O bonds, Ti–O–C bonds along with the Ti–OH groups, respectively. The presence of Ti–O–C bonds indicates that partly carbon has existed in the lattice of as-prepared TiO2 microspheres but has not replaced oxygen atom [32]. According to the above analyses, the as-prepared TiO2 microspheres turn out to be carbon-doped TiO2 microspheres (C–TiO2), which is corresponding to the valence band VB XPS results as shown in Fig. 7. Furthermore, in the Fig. 6d, a notable difference compared with the C–TiO2 sample is the presence of a peak located at 527.6eV, which may be assigned to C–Ti-O, indicating that photo-generated electrons should transfer from VB of the C–TiO2 to CQDs through C–Ti–O bond formed between C–TiO2 microsphere interface and CQDs.

VB-XPS was obtained to further investigate the property of the band structure, which can probe the total density of state (DOS) distribution in the valence band [37]. The valence band maximum (VBM) of the obtained TiO2 is determined to be 2.12eV, and the valence band tail of additional electronic states at about 0.25eV is induced ascribed to the structural disorders [38]. Notably, the result indicates that carbon atoms have doped into TiO2 and induce a broadening of the valence band and lead to the narrowing of the band gap. Thus, the obviously visible-light-response of the C–TiO2 on photodegradation can be associated to the carbon doped in lattice TiO2 corresponding to the C–Ti–O bond shown in XPS. Meanwhile, when the C–TiO2 microspheres hybrid with CQDs, the valence band maximum (VBM) consequently shift to 1.61eV whereas the band tails is at about −0.26eV. By analyzing the data, a shift of about 0.51eV of the VBM is detected upon hybrid C-TiO2 microspheres with CQDs, and the corresponding band gap narrowing values of both C-TiO2 and C-TiO2/CQDs-2 should be the same (1.87eV). Thus, the enhancement caused by CQDs might be attributed to the adsorption on the dye and restraining the recombination of the electron/hole pairs, which is associating with the band structure discussed in Fig. 7b.

UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) was employed to investigate the absorption properties of P25, C-TiO2 and C-TiO2/CQDs-2, as shown in Fig. 8. It is clearly that the spectrum of the P25 shows a significantly absorption edge at wavelength shorter than 400nm, which can be assigned to its intrinsic bandgap absorption [39]. For C-TiO2, the absorption edge (416nm) slightly shifts to the visible region with enhanced absorption intensity. The bandgap narrowing of C-TiO2 is mainly attributed to the C atoms occupying the interstitial sites in the TiO2 lattice, which leads to a decrease in optical bandgap energy. Compared with P25 and the C-TiO2 microspheres, the C-TiO2/CQDs-2 demonstrates a better absorption property in visible region (464nm), and the absorption edge appears an obvious red shift which may be ascribed to the formation of chemical bonding between C–TiO2 and CQDs, consistent with the discussion of XPS and VB-XPS. It is a direct proof that the unique hybrid structure would improve the light harvest and is also beneficial to its photocatalytic performance.

Based on the above results, the band structure of as-prepared TiO2 (C-TiO2) and carries transfer of C-TiO2/CQDs were described schematically in Scheme 1. In principle, TiO2 is a wide bandgap semiconductor and can only be excited by ultraviolet light, and the electrons may be excited from valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB) and holes are left on VB. Then the photo-induced electron-hole pairs will be separated rapidly and migrate to the surface of the semiconductor. The holes oxidize H2O and OH- to hydroxyl radical (•OH), while the electrons capture the adsorbed O2 and reduce O2 to O2 -. As is illustrated from the experimental result, the photocatalytic activities of C-TiO2 microspheres and the C-TiO2/CQDs samples are significantly improved than that of P25. First of all, the doped C species induce structural disorders in the valence band (VB) of the TiO2 and cause impurity levels, simultaneously narrow the band gap to a lower level so that the C-TiO2 can be excited under visible light irradiation. Secondly, the photocatalytic performances will be further enhanced after coupling with CQDs, because the CQDs strongly enhance the adsorption capacity of the C-TiO2/CQDs structures and act as adsorption centers, which can be found in the adsorption-desorption equilibrium (Fig. 3a). RhB molecules can strongly gather together on the surface of CQDs and be captured by •OH or O2 - and then participate in the degradation quickly. Thirdly, the chemical bonds between C-TiO2 and CQDs tend to benefit the photo-generated electrons transfer to the CQDs due to their excellent electronic conductivity, resulting in effective separation of carries.

C-TiO2 microspheres and a series of metal-free C-TiO2/CQDs have been successfully synthesized via solvothermal and in situ hydrothermal method. The carbons in C-TiO2 microspheres could lead to local energy levels in the band structure and generate valence band tails to absorb visible light. The effect of different glucose concentrations on photocatalytic activity of CQDs hybrid products was explored in detail. It was found that the C-TiO2/CQDs-2 sample obtained with a glucose concentration of 0.02%wt showed the highest degradation rate. The enhanced photocatalytic performance was attributed to the presence of CQDs, which would provide adsorption site for the pollutant molecules and serve as a charge separation center to impede the recombination and prolong the life time of electron and hole pairs. In addition, we put forwarded a facile way to synthesize carbon-doped photo catalysts, which might also provide useful information to synthesize metal-free hetero-structures for photocatalysis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 51472205 and 51302218), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of China (Grant No. 3102016ZY033), and is sponsored by Innovation Foundation for Doctor Dissertation of Northwestern Polytechnical University (NPU).
